title: QA Inertial Navigation — Zero Drift Error Budget Proof
_fixture_purpose: Proves QA T-operator navigation has zero computational drift at all step counts and noise levels. Classical INS accumulates error ~ ε√N from sin/cos float noise. QA maintains exact 0. Three routes (100, 1000, 10000 steps) × four noise levels (ULP through cheap IMU) demonstrate the error budget gap is unbounded.

witnesses.label: 10000-step route
witnesses._note: 10000 legs. Cheap IMU drift = 57m. QA drift = 0m. For submarine or underground nav, this is the difference between finding the exit and being lost.

qa_connection.theorem: For any per-step noise ε > 0 and step count N > 0: QA_error(N) ≡ 0, Classical_error(N) ~ ε·d·√N. The ratio diverges to infinity. QA is strictly superior for computational drift elimination.
qa_connection.theorem_nt: QA error = 0 because ALL computation is integer mod arithmetic. The ONLY error enters at observer projection boundaries: (1) continuous sensor reading → QA state (input), (2) QA state → continuous chart position (output). Between boundaries: exact.
qa_connection.practical_import: For GPS-denied navigation (submarine, underground, indoor, contested), DR is often the only option. Classical INS drift of ~1 nmi/hour is the fundamental limit. QA eliminates the computational component entirely, leaving only sensor noise.
qa_connection.fpga_advantage: QA navigation requires only integer multiply and modular reduction — no trig tables, no Taylor series, no lookup ROMs. An FPGA implementation uses ~10% of the logic of a classical sin/cos pipeline.

sources: INS error analysis: Savage, Strapdown Inertial Navigation (2000)
IEEE 754 ULP bounds: Goldberg, What Every Computer Scientist Should Know (1991)
